metadata.title: Precognition: Give Claude a Scratchpad Before the Answer
metadata.source: Anthropic Prompt Engineering Interactive Tutorial — Lesson 06: Precognition (Thinking Step by Step)

beats.narration_text: This is Liam for Humanitarians AI.

props.topic: HUMANITARIANS AI · Precognition: Give Claude a Scratchpad Before the Answer

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. A model generates left to right, so its first answer token becomes context that constrains the rest. Commit too early and the model rationalizes its opener rather than reasoning toward it. The fix is a thinking XML tag before the answer, a scratchpad for intermediate steps and corrections. Use it when a human expert would reach for scratch paper; skip it on simple lookups and recall.

artifactLines: A model generates left to right, so its first answer token becomes context that constrains the rest.
Commit too early and the model rationalizes its opener rather than reasoning toward it.
The fix is a thinking XML tag before the answer, a scratchpad for intermediate steps and corrections.
Use it when a human expert would reach for scratch paper; skip it on simple lookups and recall.

beats.narration_text: Here is why Precognition: Give Claude a Scratchpad Before the Answer belongs in your working knowledge. The systems, policies, and tools shaped by it affect decisions at your level — whether or not you understand the mechanism. Knowing the underlying logic gives you the frame; missing it means reasoning from output with no access to the inputs.

artifactLines: A model generates left to right, so its first answer token becomes context that constrains the rest.
Commit too early and the model rationalizes its opener rather than reasoning toward it.
The fix is a thinking XML tag before the answer, a scratchpad for intermediate steps and corrections.
Use it when a human expert would reach for scratch paper; skip it on simple lookups and recall.

beats.narration_text: The point that once the first answer token is written it constrains everything after, so the model rationalizes instead of reasons, is what I want to test myself. Help me build a fair comparison. Give me: (1) one ambiguous classification prompt written both with and without a thinking XML tag, (2) what to look for in the scratchpad that shows real backtracking, (3) a simple lookup task where adding the tag should cost tokens for no gain.

props.segment: Precognition: Give Claude a Scratchpad Before the Answer
props.command: Token-by-token generation means the first answer token constrains every token after it. If Claude answers before it thinks, that answer is already half-wrong before the reasoning starts. The fix is structural: give it a scratchpad — a thinking block before the answer tag.

beats.narration_text: A language model generates one token at a time, left to right. Once the first answer token is written, it becomes context — it constrains what can come next. Commit too early and the model rationalizes rather than reasons. The first word is already a trap.

The precognition fix is a structured scratchpad. Add a thinking XML tag before the answer tag. Claude works through the problem inside thinking — intermediate steps, contradictions, corrections — and only then writes the final answer. The scratchpad externalizes the reasoning so it is inspectable and steerable, not just implicit.

Scratchpad helps on multi-step logic, ambiguous classification, anything that benefits from backtracking. It costs on simple lookups and factual recall — extra tokens with no return. The rule: if a human expert would reach for scratch paper, add the thinking tag. Otherwise leave it out.

Your turn. Ask Claude: What breaks when you remove the thinking tag from this prompt? Pick a prompt that requires multi-step reasoning — a legal clause comparison, a math word problem, an ambiguous classification. Run it with the thinking block, then without. Watch whether the answer changes, and whether the confidence changes too. The scratchpad pattern is one line of XML. The payoff is reasoning you can read, verify, and improve. That is the precognition principle: make Claude think before it speaks. Liam, in for Bear.
